title: Git & GitHub
purpose: Track code history and collaborate through reviewable changes.
outcomes: Explain Git & GitHub concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important Git & GitHub features
Implement, test and debug practical Git & GitHub solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of Git & GitHub before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Repository, working tree and staging
topics.definition: Repository, working tree and staging is a fundamentals part of Git & GitHub concerned with how engineers deliver and operate software repeatably across infrastructure. It provides a vocabulary, operating model and constraints for making that part of a system deliberate rather than accidental.
topics.whyItMatters: Without understanding Repository, working tree and staging, a Git & GitHub solution may work on the happy path but become difficult to change, debug or operate. This topic makes ownership, behavior and failure conditions explicit.
whenToUse: Use Repository, working tree and staging when it directly supports a required Git & GitHub behavior
Use it after identifying the input, output, constraints and failure policy
Use it when the team can test and operate the resulting behavior
whenNotToUse: Do not add Repository, working tree and staging only because it is popular or appears in an architecture diagram
Avoid it when platform complexity exceeds the reliability or scale benefit
Avoid abstraction before the repeated problem and ownership boundary are understood
internalWorking: Input enters the Repository, working tree and staging boundary and is validated against its contract
Declarative configuration and controllers move runtime resources toward a desired state
State changes should preserve a named invariant and produce an observable result
Failures propagate through a defined error, retry or recovery path
tradeoffs: Correctness and clarity versus implementation effort
Runtime performance versus memory or operational complexity
Flexibility versus a smaller, easier-to-test contract
commonMistakes: Memorizing the definition of Repository, working tree and staging without tracing an example
Ignoring invalid input, failure and cleanup behavior
Choosing an advanced implementation before measuring the simpler one
topics.practicalExample: In the placement platform, apply Repository, working tree and staging to one Git & GitHub workflow such as creating an assessment, loading study content or calculating results. Define the contract, implement the happy path, reject invalid input and record enough context to diagnose failure.

topics.title: Hooks and ignore rules
topics.definition: React renders a component tree as a function of props and state, then reconciles the next tree with the previous one to update the host UI.
topics.whyItMatters: It makes interactive UI predictable by describing desired output for each state instead of manually synchronizing many DOM mutations.
whenToUse: Build stateful component interfaces
Compose reusable behavior and presentation
Represent loading, empty, success and error states explicitly
whenNotToUse: Using an effect to derive a value that can be calculated during render
Putting every state value in global context
Adding memoization before finding an actual render cost
internalWorking: A state update schedules rendering
React calls components to produce a new element tree
Reconciliation uses element type and keys to preserve or replace state
The commit phase applies DOM changes; effects run to synchronize external systems
tradeoffs: Declarative state improves consistency but rerenders require performance awareness
Local state is simple while shared state needs clear ownership
Abstraction through hooks helps reuse but can hide lifecycle behavior
commonMistakes: Mutating state directly
Using array indexes as keys for reorderable data
Omitting effect dependencies to suppress reruns
topics.practicalExample: Keep selected roadmap and search text as local state, derive the filtered list during render, and use an effect only if an external browser or network system must be synchronized.

topics.title: Bisect and worktrees
topics.definition: A data structure organizes values so required operations have predictable time and space costs.
topics.whyItMatters: The same data can be represented in ways that make lookup, insertion, ordering or traversal dramatically cheaper or more expensive.
whenToUse: Choose arrays for compact indexed access
Choose hashing for fast average membership or lookup
Choose trees or heaps when order, hierarchy or priority drives operations
whenNotToUse: Selecting from habit rather than operation requirements
Ignoring memory overhead and input limits
Using a sophisticated structure before a simple one fails constraints
internalWorking: Representation determines which locations must be visited for each operation
Contiguous storage improves cache locality
Pointer-based structures allow flexible links but add indirection
Invariants such as heap order or tree balance preserve complexity guarantees
tradeoffs: Time versus space
Fast average behavior versus stronger worst-case guarantees
Simple implementation versus specialized operations
commonMistakes: Quoting complexity without defining the operation
Ignoring amortized cost
Using recursion without considering depth
topics.practicalExample: Use a queue for breadth-first prerequisite traversal, a set for visited skills and a map from skill to dependent roadmap nodes.
